Front: When did Hitler become Chancellor of Germany?
Back: 30 January 1933

Front: Who appointed Hitler as Chancellor?
Back: President Paul von Hindenburg

Front: Why did conservative elites support Hitler?
Back: They believed they could control him and use his popularity

Front: What was the Reichstag Fire?
Back: A fire that destroyed the German parliament building in February 1933

Front: Why was the Reichstag Fire significant?
Back: It allowed Hitler to suppress political opposition

Front: What was the Reichstag Fire Decree?
Back: A law suspending civil liberties and allowing arrest of political opponents

Front: Which group was blamed for the Reichstag Fire?
Back: Communists

Front: What was the Enabling Act?
Back: A law allowing Hitler to pass laws without the Reichstag

Front: When was the Enabling Act passed?
Back: March 1933

Front: Why was the Enabling Act important?
Back: It effectively gave Hitler dictatorial powers

Front: What was Gleichschaltung?
Back: The process of coordinating all institutions under Nazi control

Front: What happened to trade unions in 1933?
Back: They were banned and replaced by the German Labour Front

Front: What happened to other political parties?
Back: They were banned making Germany a one party state

Front: What was the Night of the Long Knives?
Back: A purge of Nazi rivals in June 1934

Front: Why did Hitler target the SA?
Back: He feared its growing power and wanted army support

Front: Who led the SA?
Back: Ernst Röhm

Front: What was the result of the Night of the Long Knives?
Back: Hitler strengthened control and gained army loyalty

Front: What happened when Hindenburg died in 1934?
Back: Hitler became Führer

Front: What did the title Führer mean?
Back: Leader combining roles of president and chancellor

Front: What was the SS?
Back: An elite organisation loyal to Hitler

Front: Who led the SS?
Back: Heinrich Himmler

Front: What was the Gestapo?
Back: The secret police enforcing Nazi rule

Front: What were concentration camps?
Back: Detention centres for political opponents and minorities

Front: What was propaganda?
Back: Information used to influence public opinion

Front: Who controlled Nazi propaganda?
Back: Joseph Goebbels

Front: What role did propaganda play?
Back: It promoted Nazi ideology and Hitler's image

Front: What was the Hitler Youth?
Back: An organisation indoctrinating young Germans

Front: What was the League of German Girls?
Back: The female branch of Nazi youth organisations

Front: What was the Nazi ideal for women?
Back: Kinder Küche Kirche meaning children kitchen and church

Front: What was the purpose of the Nuremberg Laws?
Back: To exclude Jews from German citizenship

Front: When were the Nuremberg Laws introduced?
What did the Nuremberg Laws define?
Back: Who was considered Jewish under Nazi racial policy

Front: What was Kristallnacht?
Back: A violent attack on Jewish property and synagogues

Front: When did Kristallnacht occur?
Back: November 1938

Front: Why was Kristallnacht significant?
Back: It marked escalation of persecution of Jews

Front: What was Nazi economic policy?
Back: Reducing unemployment and preparing for war

Front: What public works project created jobs?
Back: The Autobahn highway construction

Front: What was rearmament?
Back: Expanding military forces

Front: Why was rearmament important?
Back: It prepared Germany for war

Front: What was the Four Year Plan?
Back: A programme to prepare Germany for war within four years

Front: Who led the Four Year Plan?
Back: Hermann Göring

Front: What was autarky?
Back: Economic self sufficiency

Front: What was Lebensraum?
Back: The idea that Germany needed more living space

Front: What territory did Hitler annex in 1938?
Back: Austria in the Anschluss

Front: What was the Anschluss?
Back: The union of Germany and Austria

Front: What agreement allowed Germany to annex Sudetenland?
Back: The Munich Agreement

Front: When was the Munich Agreement?
Which countries signed the Munich Agreement?
Back: Britain France Germany and Italy

Front: Why did Britain and France follow appeasement?
Back: They wanted to avoid another war

Front: What event began World War II?
Back: Germany invaded Poland

Front: When did Germany invade Poland?
Back: 1 September 1939

Front: Why did Britain and France declare war?
Back: They had guaranteed Poland's independence

Front: What was Blitzkrieg?
Back: A fast military strategy using tanks and aircraft

Front: What countries fell quickly to Germany in 1940?
Back: France Belgium and the Netherlands

Front: What was the Battle of Britain?
Back: An air campaign between Germany and Britain

Front: Why was the Battle of Britain important?
Back: It prevented German invasion of Britain

Front: What was Operation Barbarossa?
Back: Germany's invasion of the Soviet Union

Front: When did Operation Barbarossa begin?
Why was the invasion of the USSR significant?
Back: It opened the Eastern Front

Front: What was the Holocaust?
Back: The systematic genocide of six million Jews

Front: What was the Final Solution?
Back: The Nazi plan to exterminate European Jews

Front: What were extermination camps?
Back: Facilities designed for mass murder

Front: What happened to Germany in 1945?
Back: Germany was defeated by Allied forces

Front: What happened to Hitler in April 1945?
Back: He committed suicide in Berlin

Front: Why did the Nazi regime collapse?
Back: Military defeat economic collapse and Allied invasion
